title: Dog Constipation & Straining: When to See a Vet | Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog straining to poop? Learn about canine constipation symptoms, urgency levels, and why photos are vital for a veterinary diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Dog constipation is generally a medium-priority issue, but requires immediate veterinary attention if it persists for 48 hours or is accompanied by vomiting, bloating, or difficulty urinating.

What are the signs and symptoms of constipation in my dog?

Constipation in dogs occurs when the passage of stool becomes difficult, infrequent, or painful. You may notice your dog circling excessively, squatting for long periods, or producing small, hard, pebble-like stools. It is important to distinguish constipation from other issues, as sometimes dogs with diarrhea will also strain due to rectal irritation, a condition known as tenesmus.

When is my dog’s constipation considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for dog constipation is generally classified as medium.
  • Contact a veterinarian if your dog has not had a bowel movement in 48 hours or is visibly distressed.
  • It becomes a high-priority emergency if the straining is accompanied by frequent vomiting or a bloated and painful abdomen.
  • Seek immediate care if you suspect your dog is actually straining to urinate, which can indicate a life-threatening blockage.

How does taking a photo or video of my dog help with triage?

  • Capture a clear photo of any stool your dog has managed to produce, or even a short video of their posture while straining, to provide critical diagnostic clues.
  • Photos allow a veterinary professional to see the color, consistency, and presence of any foreign material or blood.
  • This visual evidence helps differentiate between simple constipation and more complex issues like an intestinal obstruction or impacted anal glands.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

The classic clinical signs of constipation are tenesmus and passage of firm, dry feces, which may appear thin or ribbon-like if the passage is hindered. Some animals exhibit lethargy, depression, anorexia, vomiting, and abdominal discomfort. Diagnosis is confirmed by a history of dietary indiscretion and physical evidence of retained feces. Abdominal palpation and rectal examination can confirm the presence of large volumes of retained fecal matter. Radiographs may help establish the inciting factors of fecal retention. In chronic or recurring cases, a CBC, biochemical profile (including a serum T4 level), urinalysis, and detailed neurologic examination should be completed. Consider underlying causes such as intraluminal or extraluminal obstruction, neuromuscular factors, lack of water intake, reluctance to defecate, or painful anorectal disease.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 381)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human laxatives?
faqs.answer: No, you should never administer human laxatives or enemas to your dog. Many of these products contain ingredients that are toxic to pets or can cause dangerous electrolyte imbalances.

faqs.question: How long can a dog go without pooping before it is serious?
faqs.answer: Most dogs have 1 to 2 bowel movements a day. If your dog has not produced stool for more than 48 hours, it is time to call your veterinarian for an appointment.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of constipation in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include dehydration, lack of dietary fiber, swallowing foreign objects (like bones or toys), lack of exercise, or enlarged prostate in intact males.
